speaker: interviewer
text: Today is Sunday, March 12th, 1995. The survivor is Leslie Aigner. His name at birth was Aigner, A-I-G-N-E-R. The interviewer is Merle Goldberg. The city is Portland, the state is Oregon, and the country is the United States of America. The interview will be conducted in English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My name is Merle, M-E-R-L-E, Goldberg, G-O-L-D-B-E-R-G. Today is Sunday, March 12th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Leslie Aigner in the city of Portland, the State of Oregon, the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before we begin this interview, we're doing something a little differently. It's in the evening, and we will be bringing in his children and grandchildren, because it's-- it'll be too late to bring them in at the end, the grandchildren. So we're going to start with the family. But we will also bring his two children, Susan and Robert, in at the very end with his wife because they do have some things they want to say, other than just with the grandchildren. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] I see that your family is pretty hammy. [LAUGHTER] First, would you like to introduce everyone in your immediate family to us-- your children, wife, and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Eva Erika, and this is my older daughter, Susan Marie. She's named after-- second name is named after my little sister who perished in Auschwitz. My son, Robert Maurice Aigner. He is named-- the second name, Maurice, is-- is after my father who perished in the war. And tall gentleman is Michael Johnson. He's my son in law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these two young men on your lap?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lane Johnson. My--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lane Michael Johnson!

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Johnson, seven, and this is Jeremy Robert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeremy Robert Johnson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is after his father-- his Grandpa Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four, and how-- and he's-- and Lane is seven. Since you're all together, we'll-- as I said, we're doing this a little backwards-- is there anything, now that you have all of your-- boys? Maybe you could look at me and listen to me while I'm talking. OK? OK. Good, that's great. Even though we haven't conducted the interview yet, it's been-- it's almost 50 years now since the war is over. And is there anything, now that you have them all around you, especially your grandchildren, is there anything, any lessons you've learned from the Holocaust that you'd like to pass on to your children and grandchildren? Something about being a Jew or any lesson that you might have learned from the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, surviving the Holocaust, it was traumatic, and I don't know why I was chosen to be a survivor. But the only lesson I can say, your life always have to have a goal or a hope. That's what I had all along. A hope that I will survive, nothing else. And I count my blessings every day that I have a family again, even though it's a small one. But it's beautiful to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- do you have something you want to say Lane? OK. Is there anything that-- I know that-- is it Susan or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Sue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sue, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sue, or Robert, you wanted to speak later. Is-- is there anything that anyone-- is there anything you want to say-- is there anything anyone wants to say now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I just wanted to add, I think my husband-- my message is together, that the reason we want this to be on tape, because we want the future generation to know what happens when people have hatred in their heart. And we want to make sure this never happens to our family, or to any people, ever again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. As I said, Sue and Robert will be-- will be with us, and also Mrs. Aigner, at the end. And boys, you want to look at the camera one more time so that we make sure and get your faces? And we'll be starting the interview in just a moment. Thank you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape one with Leslie Aigner. Mr Aigner, as we begin, there are some things I need to really-- just need you to say and spell for me. I would like you tell me your full name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leslie Gabor Aigner. L-E-S-L-I [LAUGHS] L-E. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gabor, G-A-B-O-R. Aigner, A-I-G-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me the-- the date of your birth, the full year and month and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me in what city and country you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I born in Czechoslovakia. Nove Zamky is the city name. Today that same area is Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Nove Zamky for us? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a piece of paper on the wall. Nove Zamky, N-- N-O-V-E dash Z-A-M-O-K.

words.text: piece of paper on the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: K-Y, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- that was Czechoslovakia at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time when the Nazi atrocity started, when I born.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I born. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born. And did you have-- and your present age right now is-- how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames at all while you were growing up, or anything like that? Or do have one now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to be called Latze, shortened Laszlo, which was my name at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Latze basically was my shortened name. But since I came to the United States, I changed the first name to Leslie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Well, what was the-- what was the name you were born with? I'm sorry, I didn't realize.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been in three different countries, so I born in Czechoslovakian, Ladislav Aigner. Then when we were taken over by the Hungarians, the territory, the town name changed to [INAUDIBLE] and my name change to Laszlo, L-A-S-Z-L-O. And when I came to the United States I changed my name again to Leslie. I hope this is the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And I think I overheard your wife calling you a nickname. And is that-- which is-- which is what? Latze, I think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Latze. Latze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Latze. Is that what people call you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just in Hungarian circles. Other than that I'm Les.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's-- I want to take you back. That's how we'll start the interview. I want to take you back to around the time you were born. I know that you probably don't remember anything about that. But if you could tell me about your parents, what your father did, what life was like-- what Jewish life was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I say, I born in Nove Zamky, a small town. Probably the population was not more than 29,000. Jewish population was about 1,000. My family lived in practically one street-- uncle, aunts, cousins, my grandmother. And we were a very close knit family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We always played together, and when it  came to holiday, we always were together. As a matter of fact, out of town family members came. But the close knit family was all in-- for this one grandma, I had six children, and I-- all on that one street together. And just the other branch of the family, my father's side, were out of town. When it came to Passover, we were 25 of us at the table, or more, sometimes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- as I grew up, as I said it was a very close-knit family, always met in the shul. And when it came to to Friday nights, we went to the Mikveh. And I-- as I grew up I went to a Jewish elementary school in Nove Zamky. And when my father had to move from the city, I still stayed back in Nove Zamky with my grandmother to finish [? eighth ?] elementary school in the same school. But because of the country change, until third elementary, I was learning in Czechish school, then it turned to Hungarian. And from then on I finished the last five years in Hungarian language. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first time I really experienced antisemitism, as I remember, when I was going to school, and back and forth to school, I've been chased and beaten because I was a Jew many, many times. There was-- started up in 1937, '38, in a very light way, atrocity, small programs in Nove Zamky. In '39, when the Hungarians came over, it-- intensified. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were always treated as different because of Jew-- my Jewishness.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was a transfer man. He was-- so, how should I say it? He had a couple of pair of horses and a two flat-bed wagon, and he was transferring people from apartment to apartment, or the merchant-- merchants had some stuff arriving to the railroad station, he was one who delivered it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1939, when the Hungarian Nazis were strong enough and they took over that part of Czechoslovakia, they took away his license. Jewish people could not have businesses. So his license was revoked, and in this little town, being a Jew, couldn't get employment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to move out of town and he moved outskirts of Budapest, a small town just 10 kilometer from Budapest, Csepel. It was the biggest industrial town in Hungary. Before-- I'm sorry, but before-- I don't mean-- I'm sorry to interrupt you. I just want to take you back a little bit. I'd like to get a little bit more information, before we get to the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to-- if you could tell me about-- tell me your parents' names, about your sisters and brothers. You know, tell me a little bit more about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, tell me about the Jewish community in Nove Zamky. You know, what the syn-- what the-- you said you went to the Mikveh. Tell me about the shuls. Tell me how-- you know, what they were like. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was-- her maiden name was Kohn Anna, and my father and mother got married in 1928. No, '27. '28, my sister, Elizabeth, born, and I born in '29. And in 1935, my small sister, Marika, born. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, we were very close and every-- very orthodox. My grandmother was very orthodox, and my mother followed to the-- down to the letter, a very kosher house. And so, as I said, we-- we were just a very close family. And we went to the shul every week. The synagogue where we went, there was probably 300 or 400-- 400 members in this little town. And naturally, on the high holidays, it always was full. Not so much in the weekends. And so it-- and there was a yeshiva where I was going after school. Until noon, until 1:00, I was going to the Jewish school, and after noon, we went to the yeshiva and studied the Torah til 4 or 5:00 in the afternoon. And as I said, many times, when we had-- when we were going home from the yeshiva, we grouped together and we dropped each other off at-- at the individual houses. I happened to be in the last house, and so that's why I was caught many times in-- by other kids who were antisemites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you illustrate-- can you explain this a little more, tell me what happened, if you were called names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were chasing me and throwing rocks at me, and if they caught me they beat me. But at the same time, when we were together and we caught one of them, we did the same thing, because we were kids. It all-- my mother always said, don't bother, don't hurt them. Try to get away from them and don't do anything bad. And so this is how we-- I'd say it was a kind of a conditioning for the future. I say it now, looking back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you-- you know, you went to shul on Friday night for Shabbos, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you paint me a picture of maybe, you know, like, your Shabbos dinner and then the shul and then the next day? Give me a little bit of what life was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our Shabbos dinner was many, many times at my grandma's house, and she was doing the ritual food, which was-- I can't even remember exactly. It was chicken in sauce and stuff like that. And it was just a nice get together with the Challah and the [INAUDIBLE] before-- before the meal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it's, as I say, this all-- all this closeness ended when-- when my father's license were taken away. As I was growing up, this was until my age 10. Then in 1939, everything started to change rapidly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I interrupted you, you said that your father-- that your father moved your family to a small town outside of Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he and my two sisters and my mother moved over to Budapest, and he got an employment over there in a paper mill. And I stayed back, and it was about three or four years later-- actually 1943, end of 1943, middle of 1943-- I moved up to Budapest. And then, I-- a higher education wasn't allowed for Jews, so my father took me to-- in Budapest to a machine shop, and told me that you're going to learn a trade. You have to do something with your life. So at age 14, in 1943, he took me to this machine shop and I started learning to become a machinist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question, though. When the war were people coming through-- people who were escaping? Did you hear things? What-- did you witness anything? Anybody come through your-- through your home to stay with you overnight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- I was too small, basically. As a-- as a 14, 15 year old boy, I don't-- didn't retain too much. I, actually I was 13-- 10. 10 to 14, when the war started to escalate, and the Nazis occupied Poland. At one time, I remember Polish soldiers coming over the border, and naturally, a short while later the Hungarian opened their borders, and then the Nazis just marched through Hungary. And so it was very little. And what I-- what we heard, I heard my folks talking about. But they were pushing kind of aside, because if I heard too much, I might blabber too much. So they didn't let me hear too much of the things. I heard things about what happening in Germany, which they took the whole news-- they took it with a disbelief. It couldn't be true that all these things can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That the Jews are being persecuted in Germany. I didn't hear exactly for-- of what happened, but we already experienced that the Jews' licenses-- business licenses-- were revoked. And-- and so we heard from-- from people, but it was a disbelief that-- that all these things can happen. And-- and they didn't tell me too much, really. They held me back because they were afraid that I will-- I will-- as a small kid, will talk in the wrong place in the wrong thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- from the years '39 to '42 or '43?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how was it, food-wise? How did you manage for food? How did you-- where did you live, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived at my grandmother's from '39 to '43, and my uncle-- I have a picture of him, too-- he was unmarried. And at that time he was 40-- in his 40s. And he and my grandma lived together, and so he was the breadwinner of the house. And he worked in an office and he kept his job and-- as an office worker. I don't know. He was a bookkeeper or something. So his job was OK, and that's how we managed. And my father got a job up in the outskirts of Budapest, and the rest of the family did the same. None of them were actually in business. They all worked. I remember one of my uncles was working for a liquor company, selling liquor. So he was travelling the towns-- small towns, the taverns, and selling the stuff. So that was his job. Food-wise it was OK in Czechoslovakia until 1939. We had basically a low-middle income families, all of us. But then when the Jews were restrained and-- and then it changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the time that you were living with your grandmother and your uncle, from '39 to '43, did you witness, during that period, anything in particular, any anti-- any antisemitic incidents? Any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many times it happened that the synagogue windows were broken in-- in high holidays. There were some small pogroms, but not-- not-- you know, it's just a group of-- of-- of antisemites [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gathering together and running away. Throwing the rocks through the window and running away. And at that time, already, the police didn't do too much about it. So they boarded up the synagogue windows, which was facing the street, and left it that way. And so it was a constant threat. But it wasn't so that every day it happened. Here and there, now and then, this happened. But it was building up. That's what I noticed between my age 10 and 14, before I moved off to Budapest. And this is why my father decided to move. Maybe we can blend in in a big city, more so, and won't be so known. Because in the small town where we lived, everybody knew who it was. So everybody knew the Jews and pointed out if it come to atrocity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were there-- in your-- were there Hitler youth in your town? Were there-- was there Gestapo? Were anything-- was anything-- were people being carted off into work camps or anything like that? Did you ever witness anything like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. In 1943, when I moved off to Budapest, then it really intensifying everything. We had to start wearing the yellow star. My father was in [? holding ?] for the forced labor camp, end of 1943. And so we stayed, we-- just four of us, my mother, my older sister, and my little sister, and myself. I was still going to trade school in the daytime. My older sister-- she was not quite 16-- she started working in a paper mill to make some living. And my father was getting as a-- because he was inducted into the forced labor camp-- there was some paycheck coming from that, very nominal. They were paying at that time. It was like an army, but it wasn't really an army. They were put out on the Russian front to dig ditches, build roads, and help the Nazi war machinery, basically, under-- under guard. They were under guard all the time. I have some letters which he wrote for my mother, which we recovered. And the letters which they exchanged-- it's in Hungarian, so it won't help too much. And they exchanged letters and I read them now, later, and I recall that my mother wrote to my father that it's-- things are very noisy here, talking about the bombardment of the Allies. And so she sent some packages for him to the different camps where he was. This was allowed to send a half a kilogram package, which was mainly cigarette and some sweets. And even wrote-- my mother wrote that we have to move in, a certain time she wrote to my father we have to move in to a new area, which was the ghetto, and states the address-- the new address for it, and he sent the letters to that address. That was end of 1943 and early 1944. And early 1944, in May, we had to start wearing the yellow star. And after end of May, they ordered us to move into the ghetto. By then, my sister was ordered into the factory. They put up some barracks situation for the Jewish workers, and she has to-- had live in there. And she was getting leaves every weekend, just for a few hours to get out to home, to change clothes, and stuff like that. And so, just three of us moved into the ghetto. My little sister, and my mother, and myself. But, as I say, my-- on weekends, my sister came home. And I have some pictures which survived in an interesting way. Just a week before, at the end of June 1944, we took these pictures with my father's camera, with the yellow star on our chest, and put it in for development. And a week later, a Hungarian Nazi-- the Arrow Cross member-- came to the door and ordered us to pack and be ready within 48 hours. And so my mother put these pictures in for development, and it was left there. And after the war, when we came back, I mentioned to my father that we had some picture taken, and this is how we-- this picture survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were with-- the fellow kept it because he saw the yellow star on it and he put it away, that maybe, someday, somebody might want to claim it. And we did. And so my father was in a work camp on the Russian front. My sister was in the factory. And we three was-- were ordered, as [INAUDIBLE], as they said, 48 hours later, trucks stopped in front of the ghetto house. And all the Jews from outskirts of Budapest, in this little town, Csepel, were shipped to another part of outskirts of Budapest, Obuda, which they had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to explain exactly where Obuda is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obuda is-- is on the north side of the Danube, which is Buda side. And we lived in Csepel, which is-- the Danube flows-- this was an island, this city. It was 10 kilometers from Budapest. And we were living in this city, which was on this island. And so, Obuda, we-- they trucked us to Obuda. We had to go over to Pest side, and then again over the the bridge to Buda side. And Obuda was outskirt of Budapest, basically, in-- in the Northeast side of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to stop here so we can change the tape. We're going to change the tape. I just-- I don't think people realize-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, people don't realize that-- how Budapest is situated, so I just wanted you to explain so people would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Splicing in two, just like Portland--
